heroDescription:
The head shrine of more than 40,000 Hachiman shrines throughout Japan.
Since its founding in 725,
a sacred place deeply revered by the Imperial Court.
aboutIntro: Usa Jingu is the head shrine of more than 40,000 Hachiman shrines throughout Japan. Since its founding in 725, it has enjoyed deep reverence from the Imperial Court and has long been esteemed as the second sacred ancestral shrine after Ise Jingu.
historyText2: The enshrined deity, revered as Hachiman Okami, has long been cherished by the people as a deity of martial fortune, culture, and agriculture, and branch shrines have been established throughout the country. The Honden (Main Hall), designated a National Treasure, is built in the distinctive Hachiman-zukuri style and represents the height of Japanese architectural beauty.
deity1Desc: The principal deity enshrined at Hachiman shrines throughout Japan. Hachiman Okami is widely revered as a deity of martial fortune, culture, and agriculture. The deified spirit of Emperor Ojin, this deity also came to serve as a spiritual pillar of bushido.
deity2Desc: This is the collective divine name for the Three Goddesses of Munakata enshrined at Munakata Taisha. Revered as protectors of the sea routes, they are believed to bestow blessings for matchmaking and harmony within the family.
deity3Desc: The divine mother of Emperor Ojin. Known through the legend of the expedition to the Three Kingdoms of Korea, Empress Jingu is revered as a deity of child-rearing and safe childbirth, embodying both strength and compassion.
legend1Text: The clear water that wells up beside the Honden (Main Hall) is said to have burst forth from the earth when Hachiman Okami first descended here. Revered since ancient times as reisui (sacred water), it is believed to purify both body and spirit and to aid the fulfillment of prayers.
legend2Text: In 749, when Emperor Shomu vowed to establish the Great Buddha of Todaiji, Hachiman Okami of Usa Jingu gave the divine oracle, "Though I am a kami, I shall protect the Buddha Dharma," and declared support for its construction. This is an important historical episode at the root of shinbutsu shugo (Shinto-Buddhist syncretism).
legend3Text: In 769, when Yuge no Dokyo sought to seize the throne, Wake no Kiyomaro visited Usa Jingu to seek the divine oracle. Hachiman Okami proclaimed, "Since the founding of our land, ruler and subject have each had their proper place. Never has a subject become ruler," thereby safeguarding the legitimacy of the imperial line.
legend4Text: Usa Jingu is regarded as one of the birthplaces of shinbutsu shugo (the harmony of Shinto and Buddhism) in Japan. The bestowal upon Hachiman Okami of the title Hachiman Daibosatsu (Great Bodhisattva Hachiman) stands as a symbol of Japan's distinctive religious culture, in which Shinto and Buddhism came together. That spirit of harmony reveals the depth and breadth of Japanese culture.

0.event: Usa Jingu is enshrined. Hachiman Okami first descends upon the land of Usa.

1.event: A divine oracle is given regarding the construction of the Great Buddha of Todaiji, marking a pivotal moment in the history of shinbutsu shugo.

2.event: The Dokyo Incident. Wake no Kiyomaro bears the divine oracle of Hachiman Okami and protects the imperial line.

3.event: Iwashimizu Hachimangu is founded through a sacred transfer from Usa Jingu.

4.event: Minamoto no Yoritomo carries out major repairs to the shrine buildings, deepening warrior devotion.

5.event: The present Honden (National Treasure) is completed, displaying the grandeur of the Hachiman-zukuri style.

6.event: The Honden is designated a National Treasure, recognized as one of the most important cultural properties in the history of Japanese architecture.

7.event: Usa Jingu marks the 1,300th anniversary of its enshrinement, with celebratory rites drawing worshippers from across Japan.

museumText: The Usa Jingu Treasure Museum houses many treasures dedicated over the course of its long history. Here you may view precious objects, including National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties, that speak to the shrine's history and culture.
museumFee: Admission: Adult ¥500 / Middle & High School ¥300 / Elementary ¥200
museumClosed: Closed: Mondays (next day if holiday), Year-end/New Year
worshipIntro: At Usa Jingu, please observe the traditional manner of sanpai (worship) handed down since ancient times. With the reverence befitting the head shrine of Hachiman, spend a quiet moment before the deities.
etiquetteSpecial: Two Bows, Four Claps, One Bow
etiquetteSpecialDesc: A special manner of sanpai preserved only at Usa Jingu and Izumo Taisha. It differs from the more common form of two bows, two claps, and one bow.

0.title: Temizu Purification
0.desc: Before sanpai, purify your hands and mouth at the Temizuya (Purification Pavilion). Proceed in this order: right hand, left hand, mouth, left hand, then the handle of the ladle.

1.title: Pass the Torii
1.desc: Bow once before passing through the torii gate. Since the center of the approach is the path of the deities, walk along either side.

2.title: Before the Haiden
2.desc: Stand before the saisenbako (offertory box) and bow lightly. Offer your coin quietly, and ring the bell if one is present.

3.title: Two Bows, Four Claps, One Bow
3.desc: Bow deeply twice, clap four times at chest height, and then bow deeply once more. Offer your prayer with sincerity.

0.name: Jogu (Upper Shrine)
0.desc: The three sanctuaries of Hachiman Okami, Hime Okami, and Empress Jingu are enshrined here. Home to the National Treasure Honden.

1.name: Gegu (Lower Shrine)
1.desc: The same three deities as the Jogu are enshrined here. This is the place for worship known as shita-mairi.

2.name: Wakamiya Jinja
2.desc: A subsidiary shrine enshrining Emperor Nintoku, the first imperial son of Emperor Ojin.

3.name: Temizuya (Purification Pavilion)
3.desc: A sacred place for purifying body and spirit before worship, where holy spring water wells forth.

4.name: Treasure Museum
4.desc: Displays precious treasures including National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties.

5.name: Omotesando (Main Approach)
5.desc: The stately approach extending from the Great Torii, lined on both sides with stone lanterns.

prayerText: At Usa Jingu, prayer rites are conducted for a wide range of heartfelt petitions. Shrine priests will offer prayers in the proper formal manner.
yakuText: We receive prayers for yakuyoke (warding off misfortune) and good fortune during yakudoshi, the traditionally critical ages. For men these are 25 and 42, and for women 19 and 33.
amuletsText: Usa Jingu offers a variety of sacred items bestowed with the divine virtue of Hachiman Okami.

0.name: Omamori
0.desc: Protective amulets for bodily safety, traffic safety, academic success, and more

1.name: Ofuda
1.desc: Sacred talismans for enshrinement in the kamidana, with prayers such as family safety and prosperous business

2.name: Ema
2.desc: Wooden votive plaques on which prayers are written and dedicated to the shrine

3.name: Goshuin
3.desc: Goshuin (shrine seals) associated with Usa Jingu. Goshuincho books are also available

4.name: Hamaya
4.desc: An auspicious arrow for warding off malevolent influences, especially popular at New Year and seasonal observances

5.name: Kamidana
5.desc: A selection of household and workplace kamidana items is available

weddingText: A graceful and solemn Shinto wedding may be held before the deities of Usa Jingu. Under the divine protection of Hachiman Okami, a new beginning is blessed.
offsiteText: Shrine priests can travel to homes and workplaces for ceremonies such as jichinsai (ground-purification rites), jotosai (topping-out rites), shunkosai (completion rites), and Shinto funerary rites.
monorailText: A monorail is available for reaching the Jogu (Upper Shrine), allowing visitors to ascend the steep sacred approach safely.
monorailNote: ※ Service may be suspended during severe weather or maintenance.
festivalsIntro: Throughout the year, many sacred festivals and rites are conducted at Usa Jingu. These traditional observances, handed down since ancient times, remain a source of spiritual support for the local community.

0.name: Gantansai
0.desc: An important New Year rite offering prayers for peace in the nation, abundant harvests, and the safety of parishioners and worshippers.

1.name: Spring Grand Festival
1.desc: One of the year's major rites, giving thanks to the deities for the coming of spring and praying for abundant harvests and public well-being.

2.name: Nagoshi no Oharae
2.desc: A great purification rite in which the defilements accumulated over the first half of the year are ritually cleansed through chinowa purification.

3.name: Hojoe Festival
3.desc: A festival distinctive to Usa, offering compassion and gratitude to all living beings. It is designated an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan.

4.name: Chokusai
4.desc: The most important festival of Usa Jingu, in which an imperial messenger attends on behalf of His Majesty the Emperor. A most solemn rite continued since ancient times.

5.name: Chushu Festival
5.desc: A grand autumn rite of thanksgiving for the harvest, accompanied by mikoshi processions and dedications of traditional performing arts.

6.name: Niinamesai
6.desc: One of Japan's oldest rites, in which the year's new grain is offered to the deities in gratitude for the harvest. Worshippers connected with agriculture visit from across the country.

7.name: Toshikoshi no Oharae
7.desc: A purification rite that removes the year's defilements so that the New Year may be welcomed with a pure heart.

scheduleNote: ※ Festival dates may be subject to change. Please contact us for the latest information.
byTrainDesc: Alight at Usa Station on the JR Nippo Main Line. About 10 minutes by taxi (approximately 5 km). Local bus service is also available.
byCarDesc: About 5 minutes from Usa IC on the Higashi-Kyushu Expressway. Several free parking areas are available along the approach.
byBusDesc: Take the Oita Kotsu bus and alight at Usa Jingu-mae. The ride takes about 15 minutes from Usa Station.
parkingDesc: Parking Lots 1 through 4 are available, with space for about 500 vehicles in total. A dedicated parking area for large buses is also provided.

0.a: Worship hours are 5:30–21:00, with slight seasonal variation. The monorail operates from 8:30 to 17:00.

1.q: Is a reservation required for prayer rites?
1.a: In principle, no reservation is required. However, larger rites such as jichinsai and weddings do require advance booking.

2.a: Yes. Parking Lots 1 through 4 provide about 500 spaces in total, and they may be used free of charge.

3.a: Yes. Goshuin are available at the shrine office from 9:00 to 16:30.

4.q: Can I bring pets to the shrine?
4.a: We respectfully ask that pets not be brought into the shrine precincts. Thank you for your understanding and cooperation.

faqPage_text_27235: Here we present questions frequently asked regarding worship and prayer at Usa Jingu, together with their answers. If you have further questions, please feel free to contact us.
faqPage_text_85789: I would like to photograph, use, or publish scenery or images for publicity or commercial purposes.
faqPage_text_35000: For questions not covered here, please feel free to contact us using the information below.
faqPage_text_67367: Why is the worship manner two bows, four claps, and one bow?
faqPage_text_99633: At many shrines, the customary form of worship is two bows, two claps, and one bow. At Usa Jingu, however, worship is offered in the form of two bows, four claps, and one bow. Although this manner is not specifically recorded in surviving documents, it has been preserved to the present day through ancient ritual tradition. Not only worshippers, but also shrine priests conducting ceremonies, perform this four-clap form of reverence.
faqPage_text_50354: Where is the Honden of Usa Jingu located?
faqPage_text_61804: Proceed to the Jogu, where the three sanctuaries stand behind the Chokushimon (Imperial Messenger Gate) before which worshippers pay their respects. Except during special viewings, worship is not normally offered from close range before the Honden.
faqPage_text_62927: How old is the great sacred camphor tree in the Jogu?
faqPage_text_89640: The great camphor tree before the Jogu prayer hall is said to be about 800 years old.
faqPage_text_33436: May I bring a pet while worshipping?
faqPage_text_28464: In order to preserve the dignity of the shrine and in light of present social conditions, worship with pets is prohibited in the areas from the Temizuya to the surroundings of the Jogu and Gegu, around Hishigata Pond leading to Goreisui, and around the monorail.
faqPage_text_22961: Pets are not permitted in the following precinct areas:
faqPage_text_24327: We kindly ask for your understanding and cooperation.
faqPage_text_79632: From the Temizuya to the surroundings of the Jogu and Gegu
faqPage_text_32062: The area around Hishigata Pond leading to Goreisui
faqPage_text_78171: I would like to request a prayer rite or purification. Is a reservation required?
faqPage_text_38384: If you are visiting individually or with family, no reservation is required. Please come directly to the reception desk of the Jogu prayer hall. If you are visiting as a group, please contact us in advance.
faqPage_text_69233: If you are visiting individually or with family, no reservation is required. Please come directly to the reception desk of the Jogu prayer hall. If a company or other organization wishes to request a prayer rite or purification, we will prepare an application form, so please contact us beforehand.
faqPage_text_38057: What is the Meoto Ishi (Married Couple Stones)?
faqPage_text_60007: They are regarded as a power spot associated with blessings for successful matchmaking. It is said that if one person steps on both stones, good relationships will be granted, while if two people each stand on one stone, they will remain in harmony for years to come. The stones are set in the stone paving of the steps leading to Wakamiya Jinja.
faqPage_text_69510: They are regarded as a power spot associated with blessings for successful matchmaking. It is said that if one person steps on both stones, good relationships will be granted, while if two people each stand on one stone, they will remain in harmony for years to come. The stones are set in the stone paving of the steps leading to Wakamiya Jinja within the precincts.
faqPage_text_75711: Is it possible to worship at the Jogu while using a wheelchair?
faqPage_text_88583: A monorail is operated for visitors with limited mobility and for elderly worshippers. As there are days when service is suspended for cleaning and related work, please contact us in advance.
faqPage_text_49580: At Usa Jingu, a monorail is operated so that worshippers with limited mobility and elderly visitors may worship more comfortably. Please come directly and make use of it. Please note, however, that service is suspended on certain days each month for cleaning work, so we ask that you contact us once before your visit.
faqPage_text_38667: Wheelchairs are available by reservation. As the number is limited, please contact us if you wish to use one.
faqPage_161618: From 9:00 to 16:00, reception is handled at the Goshuin Reception and General Information Office. From 16:00 to 18:00, only paper goshuin are available at the sacred items office.

worshipGuidePage_text_87953: The architectural style of Usa Jingu is called Hachiman-zukuri. In this form, two kirizuma-zukuri structures joined front to back are connected with a one-bay ai-no-ma (intermediate space, also called the horse passage) between them, and a large golden rain gutter spans the eaves above. These splendid buildings, with hinoki bark roofs, white walls, and vermilion-lacquered pillars, stand in a single line. The inner sanctuary holds a canopy dais, and the outer sanctuary holds sacred seats; both serve as divine seats.
worshipGuidePage_text_42262: Founded by imperial prayer during the Konin era of Emperor Saga in the 810s, the Lower Shrine was established after a divided spirit of the Upper Shrine was enshrined here. From that time, Hachiman Okami, Hime Okami, and Empress Jingu have been revered in both the Upper and Lower Shrines. This is why people say, "Without visiting the Lower Shrine, the pilgrimage is only half complete." At the Lower Shrine, Hachiman Okami manifests divine virtue in overseeing sacred food offerings and protecting the growth and fulfillment of agriculture and other industries.
worshipGuidePage_text_72556: At the foot of Mt. Kameyama beside Hishigata Pond are three sacred springs known as Goreisui. Located directly behind the Upper Shrine Main Hall on the north side, these springs have long been revered as inexhaustible sacred waters. Tradition says that in the 32nd year of Emperor Kinmei, 571, Hachiman Okami first manifested in this very place.
worshipGuidePage_text_76685: Obae no Miko / Kobae no Miko
worshipGuidePage_text_61827: Hayabusawake no Miko / Medori no Himemiko
worshipGuidePage_text_28559: Following a divine oracle in 824, this shrine was constructed and first enshrined in 852. It venerates Osasagi no Mikoto (Emperor Nintoku), the Wakamiya of Emperor Ojin, together with the imperial princes. It is especially renowned as a deity of protection from misfortune and calamity.
worshipGuidePage_text_18724: Kure Bridge is a roofed sacred bridge on the western approach. It is said to have been built by people from the ancient land of Kure, from which its name derives. The bridge has existed since before the Kamakura period.
worshipGuidePage_text_66878: South Central Tower Gate (Imperial Envoy Gate)
worshipGuidePage_text_50701: The southern main gate of the inner shrine precincts, this is the Imperial Envoy Gate and is ordinarily kept closed. It is a two-storied gate with an irimoya-style cypress-bark roof, and Koradaimyojin and Asodaimyojin are enshrined here as guardian deities of the gate.
worshipGuidePage_text_54501: This small shrine stands within the Upper Shrine, facing south to the northwest of the First Sanctuary. Regarded as a side shrine of Hime Okami, it enshrines the three creator deities believed to be the tutelary gods of the original sacred site. The shrine building is in Hachiman-zukuri style.
worshipGuidePage_text_41812: The West Great Gate is said to have been rebuilt around 1592 and has since displayed its splendid Momoyama-style form. Its roof combines kirizuma and karahafu elements with cypress-bark roofing, while the interior is especially rich in polychrome decoration.
worshipGuidePage_text_10937: This wooden torii before the West Great Gate is called the Usa Torii, and the great torii and other torii here all follow the same form. It has no gakuzuka tie-beam, and a daiwa ring is set atop the pillars. It is the standard model of the torii style of Usa.
worshipGuidePage_text_31448: This plank-built takakura storehouse houses ritual implements and related objects. It has a yosemune roof covered in cypress bark. A veranda is provided only at the front with projecting brackets and a giboshi balustrade. The raised floor supports the body of the storehouse itself.
worshipGuidePage_text_28754: The museum preserves and exhibits several hundred cultural properties connected with the shrine, including nationally and prefecturally designated treasures. It is a hall of Hachiman culture where visitors may encounter the profound history and culture of Usa.
worshipGuidePage_text_43582: The ancient lotus blooming in Hatsusawa Pond before the Treasury Museum, counted among Japan's three famed sawa ponds, is at its most beautiful from July to August. This lotus was transplanted in 1973 from a long-preserved stock kept by an old family in Kusaka, Higashiosaka.
worshipGuidePage_text_90713: The grove near the steps leading from the purification pavilion toward the Upper Shrine consists chiefly of ichiigashi oaks and camphor trees in a natural evergreen broadleaf forest. It is designated a National Natural Monument. The trees lining the main approach are also ichiigashi.
worshipGuidePage_text_21180: Long ago, an oni asked the deity for permission to devour people. The great deity replied, "If you can build one hundred stone steps to my shrine in a single night, I shall permit it." The oni piled the stones with all its might, but when it had reached the ninety-ninth step, the deity caused a rooster to crow and declared that dawn had broken. The oni is said to have cast itself into Hebori Pond in Haru. This is the legend of the Hundred Steps. Before the Showa reconstruction, the steps were rough-hewn from massive stones.
worshipGuidePage_of: Behind the Upper Shrine beside Hishigata Pond are three sacred springs. They are called Goreisui, also Okajiba, or the sacred waters of Shimoi, and are regarded as the place where Hachiman Okami first appeared. Here stands an octagonal yogoseki manifestation stone, and hoofprints are said to mark where the deity mounted a sacred horse and rose into the heavens. In the late Nara period, the shrine monk Shinsoku dug three wells before Goreisui and forged swords with this water while receiving the divine power of Hachiman Okami. One is said to be the shrine treasure known as the "Sword of Shinsoku."
worshipGuidePage_text_61272: This shrine venerates five deities who govern water. It stands on a small island in front of Hishigata Pond and Goreisui and is also affectionately called the Dragon Palace of the central isle.
worshipGuidePage_text_66186: This shrine building serves as the otabisho, the temporary resting place where the deity stays for three days and two nights during the Shinko-sai procession held each year from late July to early August. During the Showa reconstruction, the building that had stood outside the great torii was restored to the former Tongu site dating from the Oei era. Old drawings show that the Tongu for the Upper Shrine, Lower Shrine, and Wakamiya were once built on a grand scale corresponding to each respective sanctuary.
worshipGuidePage_text_29774: A child deity of Emperor Ojin, he was beloved for his devotion to learning yet yielded the position of crown prince to his elder brother Osasagi no Mikoto. He is revered as a guardian deity of scholarship.
worshipGuidePage_text_60444: These are guardian deities of shrine carpenters, temple carpenters, cypress-bark roof craftsmen, lacquer artisans, and other craftspeople from the surrounding region.
worshipGuidePage_text_84655: This shrine venerates Susanoo no Mikoto. To its west lie the former sites of the Kondo and Lecture Hall of Mirokuji, the old shrine-temple complex. In front of this shrine, the Chinyeki-sai pestilence-calming rite is held each year on February 13.
worshipGuidePage_text_37574: The hill on which the Upper Shrine stands, Ogurayama, is also called Kameyama. Kameyama Shrine venerates Oyamatsumi no Mikoto, the mountain deity of Kameyama.
worshipGuidePage_text_74918: This shrine venerates the Eight Prince Deities of Hachiman Okami. There is no shrine building; they are believed to dwell in the camphor tree by the western corridor of the Upper Shrine.
worshipGuidePage_text_81444: In 749, Hachiman Okami traveled with Hime Okami to Nara, then moved to Uwa in Iyo in 755, and after ten years returned to Usa by way of Nata-gu. At that time, an oracle declared that the deity should be enshrined on the summit of Oo-yama, one of the three sacred mountains of Usa, so a shrine was built there in 765, and Hachiman Okami was enshrined there for about fifteen years. During that period, on July 11, 769, Wake no Kiyomaro came as an imperial envoy in connection with the Yuge no Dokyo affair and received the divine teaching of Hachiman Okami to protect the imperial realm. This place is revered as that sacred site.
worshipGuidePage_text_30491: This shrine venerates Wake no Kiyomaro, who, by receiving the divine oracle of Hachiman Okami during the Dokyo affair, saved the nation from crisis.
worshipGuidePage_text_15511: This river flows through the sacred grounds of Usa Jingu. Its source lies south of Mt. Omotoyama, and its waters eventually reach Wama Beach, where the Hojoe rite has long been held, before emptying into Suo Nada. Yorimogawa is the collective name for the river: upstream from Kure Bridge it is called Yorimogawa or Kurehashi River; from Kure Bridge to the sacred bridge on the main approach it is called Tsukusegawa; and from the main-approach sacred bridge near the shrine boundary it is called Asasegawa. The name changes by location. The "upper reaches of the Usa River" mentioned in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki is this place, and the banks of Yorimogawa are rich in historic remains.
worshipGuidePage_text_26076: This is the sacred pond where Hachiman Okami manifested in the 32nd year of Emperor Kinmei. Its name comes from being enclosed by the three sacred mountains of Usa in a diamond-like form, and it has long been famed as a numinous pond.
worshipGuidePage_text_61299: Beside the Sanshuden and the Treasury Museum, this pond has long been famous as one of Japan's three renowned sawa ponds, together with Sarusawa Pond in Nara and Hirosawa Pond in Kyoto. Ancient lotus blossoms open beautifully here from July to August.
worshipGuidePage_text_39433: This is the place where purification rites are performed for ceremonies such as the imperial envoy offering festival and the Great Purification. The open space before it has long been called Okoshikake, the place where the chief priest's palanquin was set down.
worshipGuidePage_text_80508: This is the side shrine of Hachiman Okami at the First Sanctuary. Its deity, Ame no Koyane no Mikoto, also called Kasuga Daimyojin, is the revered kami who aided Empress Jingu.
worshipGuidePage_text_27553: The side shrine of Empress Jingu at the Third Sanctuary. Since 823, it has enshrined the Sumiyoshi Okami, who bestowed many forms of divine aid upon Empress Jingu.
worshipGuidePage_text_89867: The architectural style of Usa Jingu is called Hachiman-zukuri. The rear hall is called the Naiin, the inner sanctuary, and the front hall is called the Gaiin, the outer sanctuary. The inner sanctuary holds a canopy dais and the outer sanctuary holds sacred chairs, both serving as divine seats. The canopy dais is regarded as the deity's seat by night, and the chair as the seat by day.
worshipGuidePage_text_63883: At the Lower Shrine, Hachiman Okami oversees sacred food offerings and manifests divine virtue in protecting the growth and fulfillment of agriculture and general industry. Since ancient times, daily rites here have especially included prayers and thank-offerings from the people.
worshipGuidePage_text_30399: This shrine venerates Osasagi no Mikoto (Emperor Nintoku), the Wakamiya of Emperor Ojin, together with the imperial princes. It is renowned as a deity who protects against calamity and misfortune.
worshipGuidePage_text_82116: A roofed sacred bridge on the western approach, this structure has existed since before the Kamakura period. It is said to have been built by people from the ancient land of Kure, from which its name derives.
worshipGuidePage_text_13268: South Central Tower Gate (Imperial Envoy Gate)
worshipGuidePage_text_24697: The southern main gate of the inner shrine precincts. Koradaimyojin and Asodaimyojin are enshrined here as guardian deities of the gate.
worshipGuidePage_text_32712: Regarded as a side shrine of Hime Okami, it enshrines the three creator deities believed to be the tutelary gods of the original sacred site. It stands within the western middle gate of the Upper Shrine.
worshipGuidePage_text_71731: Said to have been rebuilt around 1592, this gate is one of the structures, together with the National Treasure Main Hall and Imperial Envoy Gate, that symbolizes the scenery of Usa Jingu.

worshipFortunePage_paragraph_yakudoshi_i: "Yakudoshi" refers to a period when one's fortune is believed to decline and misfortune or hardship becomes easier to encounter, yet at the same time "yaku" also carries the meaning of duty, and it has long been said that by taking up ritual or public responsibilities, one's yaku may be lifted. From the accumulated experience and wisdom of our ancestors, it was understood that life has important turning points, marked by physical and social changes that create clear transitions. At such turning points, so that one does not neglect proper preparation of heart, this custom has long been observed. Those entering such a year are encouraged, at our shrine, to receive a prayer for protection from misfortune and to spend this threshold year with a purified and serene heart.
worshipFortunePage_paragraph_yakudoshi_t: Yakudoshi includes "pre-yakudoshi, main yakudoshi, and post-yakudoshi," as well as the "returning zodiac year."
worshipFortunePage_paragraph_yakudoshi_d: "Pre-yakudoshi, main yakudoshi, and post-yakudoshi" are misfortune years that come according to age. Reckoned by traditional kazoedoshi age counting, <strong1>"main yakudoshi"</strong1> is <blue>ages 25, 42, and 61 for men</blue> and <pink>ages 19, 33, and 61 for women</pink>, while the years before and after correspond respectively to <strong2>"pre-yakudoshi" and "post-yakudoshi."</strong2> These three years of the yakudoshi period are traditionally regarded as years requiring restraint; in particular, "main yakudoshi" is also referred to as "great yakudoshi," the year when both men and women are most vulnerable to calamity and hardship due to physical and environmental changes.
worshipFortunePage_paragraph_mawaritoshi: Also, the <strong1>"returning year" (mawaritoshi)</strong1> refers to the year of one's own zodiac sign, which returns once every twelve years. Those in this year are called "zodiac men" or "zodiac women," and it is considered a shared "minor misfortune" (shoyaku) year for both sexes. However, the kanreki (61st year by traditional counting) and a man's 25th year (traditional counting) correspond to the aforementioned "main yakudoshi" years.
worshipFortunePage_paragraph_thirteen: Furthermore, the age of 13, when one encounters their first "returning zodiac year," is considered a particularly important age, and there is a custom of making a special pilgrimage known as the "Jusan-mairi" (thirteen-years-old visit).
worshipFortunePage_paragraph_toshiiwai_i: As one accumulates years, various names are given according to age. The custom of respecting longevity and offering gratitude to the deities as a special milestone celebration is called <strong1>"Shukuwaku"</strong1> (celebrative years) or <strong2>"Toshiiwai"</strong2> (celebration of age).
worshipFortunePage_paragraph_gratitude_1: "Onrei Hosai-mairi" (thanksgiving pilgrimage) refers to offering one's gratitude for the guidance and protection of Hachiman Okami when a prayer has been fulfilled, or when a year has been passed safely and happily without any disaster.
worshipFortunePage_paragraph_gratitude_2: Even when the years of misfortune—such as pre-yakudoshi, main yakudoshi, post-yakudoshi, or the returning zodiac year—have successfully ended thanks to the "prayer for protection from misfortune and opening of good fortune," worshippers make a "thanksgiving pilgrimage for the completion of the misfortune period" (yakuake no onrei hosai-mairi) to pray for continued safety and protection in the future.

aboutDeitiesPage_text_87948: Hachiman Okami [ Homutawake no Mikoto (Emperor Ojin) ]
aboutDeitiesPage_text_59419: Hime Okami [ Tagitsuhime no Mikoto, Ichikishimahime no Mikoto, Tagirihime no Mikoto ]
aboutDeitiesPage_text_43070: Beyond the Imperial Envoy Gate stands the Main Hall,
aboutDeitiesPage_text_32017: with the First Sanctuary on the left, followed in order by
aboutDeitiesPage_text_87028: From the First Sanctuary onward are enshrined Hachiman Okami,
aboutDeitiesPage_text_59127: Hachiman Okami has long been deeply revered by the imperial court for manifesting divine power on many occasions, such as the construction of the Great Buddha of Todai-ji and the divine oracle affair of Dokyo, thereby protecting the Imperial House. Not only the Imperial House, but also warriors throughout the land, beginning with the Seiwa Genji, revered Hachiman as the martial deity of "Bow-and-Arrow Hachiman," while ordinary people too cherished him as a tutelary deity. It is said that when Hachiman Okami first manifested by Hishigata Pond in the 6th century, he named himself "Homuta Emperor Hirohata Hachiman Maro" and also "Gokoku Reigen Iriki Jintsu Daijizai O Bosatsu." In the Buddhist world as well, he was venerated as Hachiman Daibosatsu, and the deity who sent the divine wind at the time of the Mongol invasions is said to have been Hachiman-sama.
aboutDeitiesPage_text_18368: True to the title "Gokoku Reigen Iriki Jintsu Daijizai O Bosatsu," he freely manifests boundless divine workings. Shrines dedicated to "Hachiman" are found throughout Japan, and with the expansive power of yaoyorozu, countless divine manifestations, he extends strong protection far and wide.
aboutDeitiesPage_text_38357: Hime Okami is said to have been born from the pledge between Amaterasu Omikami and Susanoo no Mikoto, and refers to the three goddesses Tagitsuhime no Mikoto, Ichikishimahime no Mikoto, and Tagirihime no Mikoto. Tradition says they descended to Usa-jima in Tsukushi, now Mt. Omotoyama in Usa, and they are regarded as ancient deities and tutelary land deities who were present before the manifestation of Hachiman-sama.
aboutDeitiesPage_text_50208: As guardians of the northern sea routes, they are also enshrined at Munakata Taisha and Miyajidake Shrine in Chikuzen, present-day Fukuoka, and at Itsukushima Shrine in Aki, present-day Hiroshima. Their protection extends widely, from learning and the arts to prosperity and traffic safety, including safety in navigation.
aboutDeitiesPage_text_78278: At Iwainomiya in Kashii, present-day Fukuoka, Empress Jingu received the teachings of the heavenly and earthly deities and pacified the Kumaso. Thereafter, aided by imperial ancestral divine protection, she succeeded in relations with the continent. The Nihon Shoki records her reverence for the deities and also tells how Emperor Ojin, later born in Umi in present-day Fukuoka, was still in her womb at the time.
aboutDeitiesPage_text_35028: As a mother deity, Empress Jingu grants protection for communion between deity and human, safe childbirth, education, and related blessings, and her divine virtue is held in especially high esteem.

aboutTimelinePage_text_68275: The three goddesses Ichikishimahime no Mikoto, Tagitsuhime no Mikoto, and Tagirihime no Mikoto descend to Usa-jima.
aboutTimelinePage_text_23552: During Emperor Jinmu's eastern campaign, he stayed in Usa, where Usatsuhiko and Usatsuhime built Ichihashira no Miya (Ashihito no Miya) to receive him.
aboutTimelinePage_text_20089: Emperor Ojin is born at Umi in Tsukushi.
aboutTimelinePage_text_18048: Hachiman Okami appears before Oga no Higi at the site of Goreisui in the form of a three-year-old child. (The manifestation of Hachiman.)
aboutTimelinePage_text_27197: The first shrine building for Hachiman Okami is completed. (Present-day Takai Hachiman Shrine.)
aboutTimelinePage_text_35281: By divine oracle, the shrine is moved to Oyamada. (Present-day Oyamada Shrine.)
aboutTimelinePage_text_23181: The Hayato of Osumi and Hyuga rise in rebellion. Prayers are offered at Usa-no-miya for the pacification of the uprising. Later, the Hojoe rite begins.
aboutTimelinePage_text_87335: By divine oracle, a shrine building (the First Sanctuary) is constructed on Mt. Ogurayama, and Miroku Zen-in is founded at Hiashi as well.
aboutTimelinePage_text_36091: The Second Sanctuary is established, and Hime Okami is enshrined.
aboutTimelinePage_text_24431: By divine oracle, Miroku Zen-in is moved to the west of the shrine, and Miroku-ji is founded.
aboutTimelinePage_of: Emperor Shomu dispatches an imperial envoy to pray for the construction of the Great Buddha of Todai-ji.
aboutTimelinePage_text_56299: Todai-ji in Yamato Province invites and enshrines Hachiman. (Present-day Tamukeyama Hachimangu in Nara City.)
aboutTimelinePage_text_75669: Hachiman Okami enters the capital to venerate the Great Buddha of Todai-ji.
aboutTimelinePage_text_47364: The shrine buildings are moved to Mt. Oooyama.
aboutTimelinePage_text_88973: Wake no Kiyomaro is dispatched as imperial envoy to Usa-no-miya and frustrates Dokyo's ambition to seize the throne. (The Usa Hachiman Oracle Incident.)
aboutTimelinePage_text_69528: The shrine buildings are returned from Mt. Oooyama to their present site on Mt. Ogurayama.
aboutTimelinePage_text_48266: Saicho and Kukai pray at Usa-no-miya for safe passage to Tang China.
aboutTimelinePage_text_10243: The Third Sanctuary is completed, and Empress Jingu is enshrined.
aboutTimelinePage_text_10950: The monk Gyokyo invites and enshrines Hachiman on Mt. Otokoyama in Yamashiro Province. (Present-day Iwashimizu Hachimangu in Yawata, Kyoto.)
aboutTimelinePage_text_77275: The Taira, bearing Emperor Antoku, come to Usa-no-miya.
aboutTimelinePage_text_65975: The Hachiman Usa-gu Gotakusenshu is compiled.
aboutTimelinePage_text_77400: Hosokawa Tadaoki, lord of Kokura Domain in Buzen Province, begins the restoration of the shrine buildings and festivals of Usa-no-miya.
aboutTimelinePage_text_33865: Under the policy of separating kami and Buddhas, Miroku-ji and related institutions are abolished.
aboutTimelinePage_text_26697: Usa Jingu becomes a Kanpei Taisha, an imperial shrine of the highest rank.
aboutTimelinePage_17: The Showa Great Reconstruction begins. (Through Showa 17.)
aboutTimelinePage_text_11133: Usa Jingu becomes a religious juridical person.
aboutTimelinePage_text_25117: The Hachiman-zukuri Main Hall is designated a National Treasure.
aboutTimelinePage_text_72986: The shrine precincts are designated a National Historic Site.
aboutTimelinePage_7: Re-thatching work is carried out on the Main Hall and other structures. (Through Heisei 7.)

festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_52540: Held once every ten years (most recently on October 6, 2015)
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_11440: By the will of the Emperor, there are presently sixteen shrines in Japan, beginning with Ise Jingu, known as shrines to which an imperial envoy is dispatched. At such shrines, the imperial envoy presents the Emperor's norito prayer and offers heihaku, sacred offerings of cloth, before the deity.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_72018: At this Jingu, one of those sixteen shrines, envoys for the proclamation of an enthronement known as Usa Wake-zukai, as well as regular and extraordinary prayer envoys known as Usa-zukai, have been dispatched repeatedly since Yoro 4 (720). Since Taisho 14 (1925), this has taken the form of the present once-every-ten-years Hobei Festival. On the eve of the rite, lantern processions by the citizens and worshippers of Usa, together with many other dedicatory observances, are conducted solemnly in accordance with ancient precedent.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_88529: Chineki-sai is a festival held at Yasaka Shrine, an auxiliary shrine once dedicated within the precincts as the tutelary deity of Miroku-ji. This rite is performed to expel and pacify pestilence and calamity. Following the eve festival on the previous day and the Main Hall rite on the day itself, the ceremony is conducted before Yasaka Shrine. Because it was formerly carried out at night and the Heart Sutra was chanted, it is still known as Goshingyo-e.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_42634: At the eve festival held from the evening of February 12, the Hiire no Gi, the rite of kindling the sacred fire, is performed, and divine fire is lit in the brazier prepared in the purified court for the bonfire rite. It is said that if one eats mochi roasted over this fire, one will enjoy a year free from illness and misfortune.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_37158: On the 13th, following the Main Hall rite at the Upper Shrine, the Yasaka Shrine Festival is conducted. At Yasaka Shrine, after the chief priest recites the norito prayer, the Heigoshi Shinji is performed. In this observance, a great wand called a taihei, made from a bamboo pole one jo and four shaku in length and adorned in five colors in place of sakaki branches, is cast toward Yasaka Shrine by the attendants. It is said that those who receive a taihei will remain free from illness and misfortune for a year, and many worshippers gather hoping to receive one.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_12349: The sacred performances include the dance Enbu, bugaku court dance Ryoo, and the chanting of the Heart Sutra by monks.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_50: This Chineki-sai, served in the spirit of shinbutsu shugo, was designated in 1975 as a Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Oita Prefecture in recognition of its great value as a festival rite.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_62587: This is the most important festival of Usa Jingu, performed with the bestowal of sacred offerings from the Imperial House, and is also known as the Usa Festival. It is held on the day associated with the divine manifestation of the Great Deity, the first Day of the Hare in the second month of the 32nd year of Emperor Kinmei.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_82004: After the rites at the Main Hall of the Upper Shrine have concluded, the procession of the saishu and other shrine priests who serve in the sacred rice-planting observance proceeds to the ritual field prepared within the precincts.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_38021: The water-guardian pours water into the sacred paddy and circles it three times carrying a hoe, after which the village steward, accompanied by the water-guardian, circles the field once. Amid sacred music by the shrine priests, young maidens dressed as the deity of the rice field and wearing flower hats perform the planting rite. It is a ritual of elegant grace within unaffected simplicity.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_58148: Beloved by many under names such as the Great Summer-Crossing Festival, Summer Festival, and Gojinji, this observance is formally known as the Usa Jingu Goshinko-sai, the Sacred Procession Festival. In earlier times it was also called Oharaie. Its meaning was to remove pestilence from the people, ward off calamity, and, as the head Hachiman shrine, pray for the peace and well-being of the nation and its people.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_88282: After the rites at the Upper Shrine, the sacred presences of the Three Sanctuaries are transferred from the Main Hall into three mikoshi and proceed in divine procession to the Tongu, the temporary shrine pavilion within the precincts.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_53385: The sacred procession is led by Sarutahiko, red-faced and high-nosed like a tengu, as guide of the way. Children dressed in brilliant costumes as Butterflies, Birds, and Horses form the procession. They are followed by procession officials in kamishimo and hitatare attire, lively musicians playing drum, transverse flute, and gong, the three mikoshi, and then the chief priest and shrine priests riding in palanquins.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_41362: When the mikoshi arrive at the Tongu, the sacred presences are transferred to the temporary sanctuary. Beside it, three river-gohei wands are set up at the place of purification established in the sacred court, where once they stood in the purifying stream itself. Before them, an ancient rite of purification called Suganuki Shinji is conducted solemnly with a tokinawa-gushi wand, embodying prayers for the peace of the nation, abundant harvests, and the well-being of all people.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_49280: After the sacred presences and mikoshi have remained at the Tongu for three days and two nights, they once again form a procession and return to the Main Hall of the Upper Shrine.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_91071: During the period of the Sacred Procession Festival, special stages and festival stalls are set up within the precincts, and great crowds of worshippers gather.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_59035: On the middle day of the Usa Jingu Goshinko-sai, held each year from July 31 through August 2, the Yabusame Rite is performed at the riding ground on the approach to Mt. Oooyama within the precincts, with prayers for peace under heaven, abundant harvests, and prosperity for all people.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_79062: On August 1, 2019, a Yabusame Rite celebrating the enthronement of His Majesty the Emperor was performed at this Jingu for the first time, and it has since become one of Usa Jingu's representative traditional sacred rites.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_91167: On the day, archers of the Ogasawara school of mounted archery etiquette serve in the rite together with local volunteers, including nearby students.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_76886: Three days: Sports Day in October, the day before, and two days before
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_76923: From the Nara period until 1880 (Meiji 13), this festival was known as Hojoe. Thereafter, its name was changed to Mid-Autumn Festival.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_96061: In Yoro 4 (720), in order to suppress the Hayato rebellion in Osumi and Hyuga, the Yamato court prayed to Hachiman and bore a komomakura as a divine token upon a mikoshi as they proceeded to the theater of war in Osumi and Hyuga. This palanquin is regarded as the first mikoshi in Japan.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_81875: For the pacification of the region, there was a divine progress to both provinces in the following year, and after subduing the Hayato, who resisted for three years, the deity made the return progress in Yoro 7.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_79779: At that time, the place where the heads of some one hundred Hayato were brought back and buried became the Kyoshu-zuka, located about one kilometer west of the Jingu. In addition, Hyakutayu-den, now Hyakutai Shrine, was established to enshrine the spirits of the Hayato. Further, in Jinki 1 (724), an oracle declared that a rite should be held to console the spirits of the Hayato, and in Tenpyo 16 (744), Hachiman made a divine progress to Wama Beach, where a Hojoe rite was conducted in which shellfish and snails were released into the sea to console the spirits of the subdued Hayato. This was the beginning of Hojoe.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_49015: At the Wind-Warding Festival held each year on August 7, prayers are offered for the safety of the fields and an abundant harvest. The Wind-Warding Thanksgiving Festival of October 20 is the rite of thanksgiving for the fulfillment of those prayers. Sacred performances include Shinnou and the Hokotate rite.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_52875: A himorogi sacred enclosure is set up in the audience space of the Jingu Noh Hall to invite the Great Deity, and Shinnou is performed upon the Noh stage.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_53915: The Noh principally follows the Kanze school, yet it is cherished locally under the name Usa Kanze in honor of its distinctive regional character.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_72461: From the day before the festival, parishioners erect halberds dedicated within the precincts as offerings of thanksgiving for warding off destructive winds. They are grand structures secured with great ropes.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_1123: Niiname-sai is one of Japan's traditional annual rites, performed each year on November 23 in the Imperial Palace and at shrines throughout the country. It is a festival of thanksgiving for the blessings of the kami and for abundant harvests, in which the first fruits of the new rice are offered before the deity. In the Imperial Court, His Majesty the Emperor offers the new grain to the kami and partakes of it himself. The first Niiname-sai performed after an emperor's enthronement is called the Daijosai.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_217: As a counterpart to Niiname-sai, there is also each year on February 17 the Kinen-sai, a rite praying for the year's abundance.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_11527: On this day, many offerings of newly harvested rice dedicated by parishioners and worshippers are presented, and together with the worshippers thanks are given for the abundance of the five grains.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_27026: many sacred festivals are conducted throughout the year.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_44851: These traditional observances, handed down from ancient times,
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_26336: remain a spiritual support for the hearts of the local people.
festivalsFestivalDetailPage_text_39206: From the list below, you may go directly to each festival.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_50639: A festival celebrating the New Year and praying for the flourishing of the Imperial House, the prosperity of the nation, and also the peace and prosperity of parishioners, worshippers, and society.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_63491: Like the Saitan-sai, this festival prays for peace in the Imperial House and the nation.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_56798: A festival observed at shrines throughout the country in accordance with the rite personally presided over by His Majesty the Emperor at the Three Palace Sanctuaries, celebrating the origin of the imperial throne.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_56457: A festival celebrating the sacred birth of Hachiman Okami, Emperor Ojin.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_37390: A festival praying for the health, freedom from illness, and safety in construction and repair work for the craftsmen who serve Usa Jingu, including carpenters and lacquerers.
festivalsFestivalListPage_10: Setsubun originally referred to the Chinese rite of tsuina performed on New Year's Eve to drive away the demons of pestilence. At this Jingu, soybeans are offered together with the sacred food offerings at the Setsubun Festival conducted at 10 a.m.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_78806: A festival established on the day regarded in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki as the enthronement day of Emperor Jinmu, the first emperor, praying for the flourishing of the Imperial House and the peace of the nation.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_47450: A rite held on the day before Chineki-sai, marking the beginning of the sacred burning-offering observances. Sacred fire is kindled in five-colored small wands, and old talismans gathered within the fence of Yasaka Shrine are reverently burned.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_84489: A festival for dispelling and pacifying pestilence and calamity. Following the eve rite and the Main Hall rite on the day itself, the ceremony is held before Yasaka Shrine. It includes the Heigoshi rite, the dance of Ryoo, and the Hatogae rite.
festivalsFestivalListPage_217: A festival praying for abundant grain, the flourishing of industry, and the peace of the nation. Since the Meiji period, it has been observed on February 17.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_36352: A festival celebrating the birthday of His Majesty the Emperor and praying for his long life and the enduring prosperity of the imperial reign.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_27457: A festival held on the sacred anniversary of the divine manifestation of Hachiman Okami.
festivalsFestivalListPage_9157: Seven days, from the 9th through the 15th
festivalsFestivalListPage_7: A festival of ritual purification and abstinence. Ceremonies are conducted each day for seven days. On the night before the first day, the Shibasashi rite is performed.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_88612: The most important festival of Usa Jingu. Sacred offerings are bestowed from the Imperial House, and it is conducted with the highest ceremonial form, the Grand Rite.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_37158: A festival recalling the passing of Hachiman Okami, Emperor Ojin, and receiving anew his divine virtue.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_23030: A festival conducted with the gathering of many members of Hachiman confraternities from around the country. Prayers are offered for their peace and well-being.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_91508: In addition to the usual sacred offerings, cherry blossoms from within the precincts of Usa Jingu are presented. After the chief priest recites the norito prayer, shrine maidens offer the Hoei no Mai while holding cherry branches.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_31042: The day of Emperor Showa's birth. It is a festival that honors his sacred virtues and great achievements, and prays for the flourishing of imperial majesty and the prosperity of the nation.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_19995: After the Main Hall rite at the Upper Shrine, the Saiden Festival is conducted at the ritual ground beside Hishigata Pond. The village steward, water-guardian, and sacred maidens perform the rice-planting rite amid ceremonial music.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_60134: A rite that purifies and cleanses the sins and impurities that we and society may unknowingly have committed over the past half year. Using special ritual implements such as the tokinawa-gushi, kirinusa, and torn cloth, purification is performed for the shrine priests, those in attendance, and worshippers from all over the country by means of katashiro substitutes sent in.
festivalsFestivalListPage_123: Festival service corps assembly, Gohatsuren-sai, Suganuki Shinji, and Gochakuren-sai (Day 1); Asamikesai, Yabusame Rite, and Yumikesai (Day 2); Asamikesai, Gokanko-sai, and Gokanchaku-sai (Day 3)
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_39041: Held on the middle day of the Sacred Procession Festival (Nagoshi Festival), this rite is conducted at the riding ground on the approach to Mt. Oooyama within the precincts, praying for peace under heaven, abundant harvests, and prosperity for all people. This observance began as a celebratory rite for the enthronement of His Majesty the Emperor in 2019 and has since become an annual rite. It is served by the Ogasawara school of mounted archery etiquette.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_17275: The Mushifuri Festival was originally a rite for airing the robes and treasures kept within the shrine halls. The Wind-Warding Festival is a rite praying for the safe growth of rice and crops during August, when they are especially vulnerable to wind, flood, blight, and insect damage.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_35126: The mikoshi of the First Sanctuary, bearing Hachiman Okami, proceeds to Ukiden at Wama Shrine. A Hojoe rite is conducted in which snails and shellfish are released upon Wama Beach to console the spirits of the Hayato of Osumi and Hyuga.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_84480: Gohatsuren-sai and Gochakuren-sai (Saturday); Ninakari-sai, Suijin-sai, Shioya-sai, Mid-Autumn Festival, Hojoe Rite, and Yumikesai (Sunday); Asamikesai, Gokanko-sai, and Gokanchaku-sai (Monday)
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_11924: A festival offering heartfelt congratulations on the day of the Kannamesai at Ise Jingu. New rice from that year is offered from the Imperial House to Ise Jingu, and thanks are given for the rice harvest while its joy is shared together.
festivalsFestivalListPage_87: A festival of thanksgiving for the fulfillment of prayers made at the Wind-Warding Festival on August 7 for the safety of the fields and an abundant harvest. As sacred entertainments, it includes the Hokotate rite and Shinnou.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_73909: Held on the Noh stage as part of the sacred entertainments of the Wind-Warding Thanksgiving Festival. It chiefly follows the Kanze school among the four schools of Noh, and is especially known as Usa Kanze. It is offered mainly by parishioners of Usa Jingu, though by longstanding precedent the play Kiyotsune alone is never offered.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_91074: The day of Emperor Meiji's birth. It is a festival that honors his sacred virtues and great achievements, praying for the flourishing of imperial majesty, the development of the nation, the advancement of culture, and peace and prosperity.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_26801: A rite of thanksgiving to the deities for the year's harvest, while also praying for the peace and prosperity of the Imperial House, the nation, and the people. In the Imperial Court, food and sacred sake made from the new grain are offered to the heavenly and earthly deities, and His Majesty the Emperor partakes of them himself.
festivalsFestivalListPage_15217: Seven days, from the 15th through the 21st
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_66400: Reisai for the Transfer of Spirit into Talismans and Amulets
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_50239: A festival held before the New Year in which the divine sign of the deity is enshrined into newly prepared talismans, amulets, hamaya arrows, zodiac ornaments, and other auspicious objects.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_47409: Same as the Nagoshi Great Purification.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_70659: The final festival of the year. It is a rite of thanksgiving for having passed the year safely, while purifying mind and body so that the New Year may be welcomed with renewed spirit.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_76925: A festival observed regularly each month.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_54922: A monthly regular festival of Omoto Shrine.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_39344: Remote Worship of the Showa Emperor Festival
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_21709: The day of Emperor Showa's passing, observed at the Musashino Mausoleum in the Imperial Court.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_20669: Remote Worship of the Spring Imperial Spirits Festival
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_24515: The Spring Imperial Spirits Festival is an ancestral rite of the Imperial House held at the Koreiden in the Imperial Court on the day of the spring equinox.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_29101: Remote Worship of the Emperor Jinmu Festival
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_77986: The day of Emperor Jinmu's passing, observed at the Unebiyama no Eshihokuryo Mausoleum in the Imperial Court.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_35483: Remote Worship of the Autumn Imperial Spirits Festival
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_26760: The Autumn Imperial Spirits Festival is an ancestral rite of the Imperial House held at the Koreiden in the Imperial Court on the day of the autumn equinox.
festivalsFestivalListPage_of: A festival of thanksgiving for abundant grain, conducted at Ise Jingu. In the Imperial Court, the Kanname Hoshuku Festival is held following the Kannamesai of Ise Jingu.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_82241: The enshrined deity is Takenouchi no Sukune no Mikoto. He served as minister to Emperors Keiko, Seimu, Chuai, Ojin, and Nintoku, and is beloved by many worshippers as a deity of learning, loyalty, and martial virtue.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_13170: The enshrined deity is Oyamazumi no Mikoto. Kameyama Shrine is the mountain deity and tutelary land deity that guards Mt. Ogurayama, where Hachiman Okami is enshrined. From ancient times until the Meiji period, divination using turtle shells called kiboku was performed, and the shells used there are said to have been buried at Kameyama Shrine.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_56702: The enshrined deity is the spirit of Menegi Junior Fourth Rank, Oga no Ason Morime. Although no shrine building remains today, the festival is still conducted.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_39649: The enshrined deity is Ame no Koyane no Mikoto. By divine oracle of Amaterasu Omikami, this deity is enshrined as the side sanctuary of the First Hall, where Hachiman Okami is worshipped.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_89252: The enshrined deities are Hachiman Okami, Hime Okami, and Empress Jingu. Because it was the place where food offerings were prepared, it was also called Mikeden, the Sacred Kitchen Hall.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_33508: The enshrined deities are Ohosasagi no Mikoto, Oobae no Miko, Koboe no Miko, Hayabusawake no Miko, and Medori no Hime Miko. Ohosasagi no Mikoto is the divine spirit of Emperor Nintoku and has long been revered as a martial deity.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_23612: The enshrined deity is Uji no Wakiiratsuko no Mikoto, a prince of Emperor Ojin and a deity of learning.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_45063: The enshrined deities are the spirits of the Hayato of Osumi and Hyuga. In Yoro 4 (720), the Yamato court prayed to Hachiman Okami and proceeded to the battlefield in Osumi and Hyuga to suppress the rebellion of the Hayato there. In accord with Hachiman Okami's divine will, which hates the sin but not the person, the subdued spirits of the Hayato were consoled and enshrined.
festivalsFestivalListPage_810: The enshrined deities are Hachiman Okami, Hime Okami, and Empress Jingu. It lies 8 kilometers from Usa Jingu, and during the Mid-Autumn Festival in October, the sacred presence is transferred from the Main Hall to a mikoshi and proceeds there in divine procession.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_31439: The enshrined deity is Hachiman Okami. This is the place where Lord Wake no Kiyomaro received the divine oracle during the Usa Hachiman Oracle Incident of Jingo Keiun 3 (769).
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_46321: The enshrined deity is Wake no Kiyomaro Ason no Mikoto. In the Usa Hachiman Oracle Incident, the Imperial House was protected through Lord Wake no Kiyomaro's spirit of utmost sincerity and courageous action.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_69886: The enshrined deity is Usatsuhiko no Mikoto. The deity is enshrined in the First Hall of the Tongu. Those bearing the Usa surname, who trace their ancestry to Usatsuhiko no Mikoto, still take part in the festival today.
festivalsFestivalListPage_1128: The enshrined deities are Emperor Ojin, Emperor Chuai, and Empress Jingu. Takai Hachiman Shrine is said to be the first sacred place where a shrine of Hachiman Okami was founded. It remains deeply revered as the tutelary shrine of the local community. Its annual festival is held on November 28.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_27072: The enshrined deity is the spirit of the venerable Oga no Higi, to whom Hachiman Okami first manifested. He is worshipped in the Lower Shrine's First Hall as a deity sharing the sanctuary. Those of the Oga surname still take part in the festival today.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_20841: The enshrined deities are Hachiman Okami, Hime Okami, and Empress Jingu. This is the annual festival of Omoto Shrine, the inner shrine of Usa Jingu. After the rites conclude, mochi is scattered, and the occasion is lively every year.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_76808: The enshrined deities are Taokihooi no Mikoto, Hikosashiri no Mikoto, and Omoikane no Mikoto. It is said that this shrine was invited and enshrined in response to the wishes of carpenters, bark roofers, lacquerers, and other craftsmen from nearby regions and within Usa Jingu. The festival prays for safety in construction and repair work for the artisan trades.
festivalsFestivalListPage_43: The enshrined deities are Ame no Minakanushi no Kami, Takamimusubi no Kami, and Kamimusubi no Kami. They are tutelary land deities of Usa Jingu and are enshrined in the side sanctuary of the Second Hall. The shrine is said to preserve the prototype of the Hachiman-zukuri style of the Main Hall and was designated a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property in 1968.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_14093: The enshrined deities are Uwatsutsu no Ono no Mikoto, Nakatsutsu no Ono no Mikoto, and Sokotsutsu no Ono no Mikoto. They are revered as guardian deities of maritime safety, safe navigation, and abundant catches. It is said that when Empress Jingu prayed for victory over a foreign land, she received the aid of these deities, who are now worshipped in the side sanctuary of the Third Hall.
festivalsFestivalListPage_213: The enshrined deity is Susanoo no Mikoto. Before the Meiji period it was also called Gion-sha and served as the guardian shrine of Miroku-ji. On February 13, the Chineki-sai, which dispels and pacifies pestilence and calamity, is conducted here.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_22688: The enshrined deity is Sugawara no Michizane Ason no Mikoto. Revered as a deity of learning, he is said to have been invited and enshrined through the deep devotion of many parishioners of the time.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_31128: The enshrined deities are Takaokami no Kami, Ame no Mikumari no Kami, Ame no Kuhizamochi no Kami, and Kuni no Kuhizamochi no Kami. As deities governing water, they are enshrined in the shrine standing at the center of Hishigata Pond.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_22514: The enshrined deities are Omononushi Okami and Emperor Sutoku. Revered as deities of navigation, they are said to have been invited and enshrined through the deep devotion of many parishioners of the time.
festivalsFestivalListPage_49: The enshrined deities are Emperor Ojin, Emperor Chuai, and Empress Jingu. As in the spring festival on April 9, many parishioners attend every year.
festivalsFestivalListPage_63: The enshrined deity is Amaterasu Omikami. In 1988, this shrine was merged into Yasaka Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Usa Jingu, from the Yozan Shrine formerly enshrined within Usa City.
festivalsFestivalListPage_33: The enshrined deities are thirty-three sacred stones, or the deities known as the Hachioji. They are also said to be attendant deities of the First, Second, and Third Main Sanctuaries, though the details are unknown. There is no shrine building; instead, the sacred tree, a camphor at the western edge within the tamagaki fence, serves as the yorishiro.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_67689: Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrine Annual Festivals
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_25623: festivals and sacred rites, great and small, are conducted each year.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_91075: all are solemn and valorous observances of deep tradition.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_54621: Festivals observed at fixed times each year as annual rites.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_40975: Regular rites observed on fixed days each month.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_87626: Rites of reverent worship offered from afar
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_60897: for festivals conducted in the Imperial Court or at other shrines.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_50496: At the auxiliary and subordinate shrines of Usa Jingu,
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_67016: their respective annual festivals are conducted once each year.
festivalsFestivalListPage_text_22178: Here we introduce the enshrined deities of each shrine and their histories.

faqPage_intro: Here are the most frequently asked questions and answers regarding worship and prayer at Usa Jingu. If you have any inquiries, please feel free to contact us.
faqPage_photoQ: I would like to take, use, or publish scenery photos and images for public relations or commercial purposes.
faqPage_contactText: For inquiries regarding matters not listed here, please feel free to contact us below.
faqPage_q_reisai: Why is the worship etiquette "2 bows, 4 claps, 1 bow"?
faqPage_a_reisai: Usually Shinto worship is "2 bows, 2 claps, 1 bow," but Usa Jingu follows the traditional ancient rite of "2 bows, 4 claps, 1 bow" passed down for generations.
faqPage_q_honden: Where is the Main Sanctuary (Honden) located?
faqPage_a_honden: The three main halls are built side-by-side behind the Chushimon gate at the Upper Shrine. Except for special viewings, visitors normally worship from the gate.
faqPage_q_kusunoki: How old is the Sacred Camphor Tree (Goshinboku) in the Upper Shrine?
faqPage_a_kusunoki: The giant camphor tree in front of the Upper Shrine prayer hall is said to be about 800 years old.
faqPage_q_pet: Are pets allowed within the shrine grounds?
faqPage_a_pet: Pets are prohibited in the sacred areas including the path from Temizuya to Upper/Lower shrines, around the Hishigata Pond, and on the monorail.
worshipMonorail_text_3: 清掃作業のため、毎月運行を停止する日があります。
worshipMonorail_text_5: 危険防止のため着席または手すりにおつかまりください。
worshipMonorail_text_6: モノレールは、悪天候及び整備点検等により一時運行できない場合があります。

layout_text_2: 宇佐神宮 - 八幡総本宮
layout_text_3: 全国四万社あまりの八幡様の総本宮、宇佐神宮。神亀二年（725年）の創建以来、皇室の御崇敬篤き霊地。
home_text_2: 八幡総本宮 宇佐神宮
home_text_3: 全国四万社あまりの八幡様の総本宮、宇佐神宮。神亀二年（725年）の創建以来、皇室の御崇敬篤き霊地。
home_text_7: 大分県宇佐市にある神社。全国の八幡宮の総本社であり、国宝の本殿や美しい自然、伝統的な参拝作法である二礼四拍手一礼で知られています。

faq_text_3: Here are the most frequently asked questions and answers regarding worship and prayer at Usa Jingu. If you have any inquiries, please feel free to contact us.
faq_text_4: I would like to take, use, or publish scenery photos and images for public relations or commercial purposes.
faq_text_7: For inquiries regarding matters not listed here, please feel free to contact us below.
faq_text_31: Why is the worship etiquette "2 bows, 4 claps, 1 bow"?
faq_text_32: Usually Shinto worship is "2 bows, 2 claps, 1 bow," but Usa Jingu follows the traditional ancient rite of "2 bows, 4 claps, 1 bow" passed down for generations.
faq_text_33: Usually, Shinto worship etiquette is "2 bows, 2 claps, 1 bow," but at Usa Jingu, we follow the ancient tradition of "2 bows, 4 claps, 1 bow." Although not documented in text, this custom has been preserved through ritual tradition. Shinto priests also perform the 4-clap ritual during official ceremonies.
faq_text_34: Where is the Main Sanctuary (Honden) located?
faq_text_35: The three main halls are built side-by-side behind the Chushimon gate at the Upper Shrine. Except for special viewings, visitors normally worship from the gate.
faq_text_36: Once you proceed to the Upper Shrine, the three sanctuary halls are built side-by-side behind the Chushimon gate. Except for special designated public viewings, visitors normally worship from the front gate rather than entering the sanctuary directly.
faq_text_37: How old is the Sacred Camphor Tree (Goshinboku) in the Upper Shrine?
faq_text_38: The giant camphor tree in front of the Upper Shrine prayer hall is said to be about 800 years old.
faq_text_39: The giant sacred camphor tree standing in front of the Upper Shrine prayer hall is estimated to be approximately 800 years old.
faq_text_40: Are pets allowed within the shrine grounds?
faq_text_41: Pets are prohibited in the sacred areas including the path from Temizuya to Upper/Lower shrines, around the Hishigata Pond, and on the monorail.
faq_text_42: From the Temizuya purification font up to the Upper and Lower Shrines
faq_text_43: Around the Hishigata Pond leading to the Sacred Springs (Goreisui)
faq_text_45: Do I need a reservation for prayers and blessing rituals?
faq_text_46: No reservation is needed for individuals and families. For companies or large groups, please contact us in advance.
faq_text_47: Reservations are not required for individuals and families. Please come directly to the Upper Shrine Prayer Hall reception desk. If you wish to request a ritual for companies, corporate entities, or large groups, please contact us in advance so we can prepare the necessary forms.
faq_text_48: What are the "Husband and Wife Stones" (Meoto-ishi)?
faq_text_49: A popular spiritual spot for finding a good match. Standing on them is said to bring good relationships and long marriages.
faq_text_50: They are known as a spiritual "power spot" that brings blessings for good relationships and marriage. If visiting alone, stepping on both stones is said to bring a good match; if visiting as a couple, stepping on them together is said to grant a harmonious and long-lasting marriage. They are located in the stone pathway leading to the Wakamiya Shrine.
faq_text_51: Is the Upper Shrine accessible for wheelchair users?
faq_text_52: Yes, a monorail is operated for elderly and disabled visitors. Please check in advance as there are monthly maintenance closures.
faq_text_53: To ensure all visitors, including disabled and elderly guests, can comfortably worship, Usa Jingu operates a passenger monorail up to the Upper Shrine. Please feel free to use it. Please note that the monorail is closed for maintenance on designated days each month, so we advise inquiring beforehand.
faq_text_55: Yes, wheelchair rental is available by reservation. Please contact us as the number of chairs is limited.
faq_text_56: Yes, we offer wheelchair rentals on a reservation basis. Since the number of wheelchairs is limited, please contact us in advance to check availability.
faq_text_57: What are the hours for receiving Goshuuin (shrine seal stamps)?
faq_text_58: Shrine stamps are available at the main counter from 9:00 to 16:00. From 16:00 to 18:00, pre-written sheets are available at the amulets counter.
faq_text_59: From 9:00 to 16:00, Goshuuin are accepted and hand-written at the Goshuuin / Information Counter. From 16:00 to 18:00, only pre-written stamp sheets are distributed at the amulets counter.
worshipGuide_text_3: こくほう ほんでん
worshipGuide_text_4: 三之御殿　神功皇后
worshipGuide_text_5: 二之御殿　比売大神
worshipGuide_text_6: 一之御殿　八幡大神
worshipGuide_text_7: 宇佐神宮の建築様式は八幡造(はちまんづくり)とよばれています。この八幡造は、二棟の切妻造平入の建物が前後に接続した形で、両殿の間に一間の相の間（馬道）がつき、その上の両軒に接するところに大きな金の雨樋(あまどい)が渡されています。桧皮葺(ひはだぶき)で白壁朱漆塗柱の華麗な建物が、横一列に並んでいます。内院には御帳台があり、外院には御椅子が置かれ、いずれも御神座となっています。
worshipGuide_text_10: 嵯峨天皇の弘仁年間（８１０年代）勅願によって創建され、上宮の御分神をご鎮祭になったことがきっかけで、八幡大神様・比売大神様・神功皇后様は上下御両宮のご鎮座となりました。「下宮参らにゃ片参り」と云われる所以です。下宮の八幡大神は、御饌(みけ)を司るとともに、農業や一般産業の発展、充実をお守りになるご神威を発揮されます。
worshipGuide_text_13: 亀山の麓、菱形池のほとり、三つの霊泉からなるこの御霊水は、上宮御本殿の真裏（北側）に位置し、往古、常に清水が湧き出で絶えることのない霊泉として知られています。欽明天皇三十二年（５７１）、八幡大神がこの御霊水の辺りに初めてご顕現になったと伝えています。
worshipGuide_text_18: 大葉枝皇子・小葉枝皇子
worshipGuide_text_20: 天長元年（８２４）ご神託があって、仁壽２年（８５２）に造営創祀されました。応神天皇の若宮であられる大鷦鷯命（仁徳天皇）と皇子をお祀りしています。除災難・厄難の神様として有名です。
worshipGuide_text_24: 呉橋は西参道にある屋根がついた神橋です。昔、呉の国の人が掛けたともいわれ、この名があります。鎌倉時代より以前からある橋です。
worshipGuide_text_27: みなみちゅうろうもん
worshipGuide_text_30: 神宮内郭の南正門。勅使門で通常は開かずの門です。入母屋造桧皮葺楼門、高良大明神、阿蘇大明神の二神を御門の神としてお祀りしています。
worshipGuide_text_37: 神宮の上宮内、第一殿の西北に南面して祀られる小社で、比売大神の脇殿といわれ、本宮の地主神と伝えられる造化三神を祀っています。社殿は八幡造です。
worshipGuide_text_41: 西大門は文禄のころ（１５９２～）改築されたといわれ、以来この桃山風の華麗な構造となっています。屋根は切妻及び向唐破風造りで桧皮葺、内部はとくに極彩色が多用されています。
worshipGuide_text_45: 西大門前のこの木造鳥居は宇佐鳥居と称し大鳥居をはじめ他の鳥居はすべてこれと同じ形式のものです。額束はなく、台輪を柱上に置いています。宇佐の鳥居の規格となるものです。
worshipGuide_text_49: 祭器具等を納める高倉の板倉です。寄棟造り桧皮葺。前面のみ持送りで縁を設け、擬宝珠高欄を備えています。床上が倉の本体です。
worshipGuide_text_52: 当宮関係の国指定文化財、県指定文化財等数百点の文化財を収蔵、展示公開しています。深遠な宇佐の歴史と文化に触れることの出来る八幡文化の殿堂です。
worshipGuide_text_55: 日本三沢の池に数えられる、宝物館前の『初沢の池』に咲く古代蓮が最も美しく咲くのは７月から８月。この蓮は、東大阪市日下町の旧家に伝わっていたものを１９７３年に移植したものです。
worshipGuide_text_58: 手水舎前の参道から上宮へ向かう階段付近に群生する木々は、イチイガシと楠を主体とした自然のままの常緑広葉樹林で、国の天然記念物に指定されています。表参道の並木もイチイガシです。
worshipGuide_text_61: 大昔に鬼がいて、「人を喰べていいか」と神に許しを乞うたところ、大神は、「わが宮の石段百段を一夜に築いたならば許してやろう」と言われました。鬼は懸命に石段を積みましたが、９９段目まで積み上げたとき、大神は、鶏を鳴かせて夜が明けたと告げられました。鬼は原(はる)の蛇堀の池に身を投げたといいます。これは百段にまつわる伝説です。昭和造営前の石段は大石を使った荒造りでした。
worshipGuide_text_64: 上宮の裏、菱形池のほとりに三つの霊泉があります。御霊水(ごれいすい)、または御鍜治場(おかじば)、下井の霊水とも言い、八幡大神が御現れになったところであるとされています。ここには八角の影向石(ようごうせき)があり大神が神馬に召され、天翔けられたと伝えられる馬蹄の跡があります。また、奈良朝の末ごろ、社僧の神息(しんそく)が御霊水の前に三個 of 井戸を掘り、この水で八幡大神の神威を頂いて刀を鍛えました。これが社宝となっている『神息の刀』と伝えられています。
worshipGuide_text_68: 高龗神（たかおかみのかみ）
worshipGuide_text_69: 天水分神（あめのみくまりのかみ）
worshipGuide_text_70: 国水分神（くにのみくまりのかみ）
worshipGuide_text_71: 天汲匏持神（あめのくはざもちのかみ）
worshipGuide_text_72: 国汲匏持神（くにのくはざもちのかみ）
worshipGuide_text_73: 水を司る五神を祀る神社です。菱形池、御霊水前の小島に鎮座され、中島の竜宮様とも申し上げます。
worshipGuide_text_76: 七月末、八月上旬毎年行われる神幸祭に、三日二夜の間御滞在になる御旅所(おたびしょ)に当る社殿です。昭和御造営に臨み、大鳥居の外側にあった社殿を応永年間の頓宮旧蹟に復されました。昔の造替の時の上宮、下宮、若宮の頓宮は、当時の各社殿に準じ、大きな規模であったことが古図に残っています。
worshipGuide_text_80: 菟道稚郎子命（うじのわきいらつこのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_81: 応神天皇の御子神で、勉学に励み寵愛されていましたが、兄の大鷦鷯命に皇太子の座を譲りました。学問の神としてご守護くださいます。
worshipGuide_text_83: もくしょうそじんじゃ
worshipGuide_text_85: 手置帆負命（たおきほいのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_86: 比古狭知命（ひこさしりのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_87: 宮大工・寺大工・桧皮師・塗師の職人達と、近郷近住の職人の守護神です。
worshipGuide_text_91: 須佐之男命（すさのおのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_92: 須佐之男命(すさのおのみこと)をお祀りしています。この神社の西側に昔の神宮寺弥勒寺の金堂や講堂の旧蹟があります。この神社の前で、毎年二月十三日に鎮疫祭(ちんえきさい)が行われます。
worshipGuide_text_96: 大山祇命（おおやまつみのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_97: 上宮が鎮座している小椋山(おぐらやま)は亀山といいます。亀山神社は、亀山の山の神である大山積命(おおやまつみのみこと)を祀る神社です。
worshipGuide_text_100: 八幡大神の八王子神をお祀りしております。社殿の構えはなく、上宮、西回廊の楠の木に鎮まっておられます。
worshipGuide_text_105: 天平勝宝元年（７４９）八幡大神は比売大神とともに奈良に行幸、天平勝宝７年（７５５）伊予の宇和に移り、１０年後奈多宮を経由して宇佐に御帰還になりました。その時、宇佐三山の一つ大尾山の頂上に御鎮座するとの託宣があったので、天平神護元年（７６５）に造営し、八幡大神様はここに約１５年間鎮座されました。この間の神護景雲３年（７６９）７月１１日、和気清麻呂公が弓削道鏡の事件に際して勅使として参拝され、八幡大神様より国体擁護の御神教を授かった霊地です。
worshipGuide_text_110: 道鏡事件の際、八幡大神様の神託を受けて国の危機を救った和気清麻呂をお祀りしています。
worshipGuide_text_113: 宇佐神宮の神域を流れる川で、源は御許山の南にあり、流れの末端は、古くから放生会を行っている和間の浜で、周防灘に注いでいます。寄藻川は、この川の総称で、呉橋から川上を寄藻川、また呉橋川といい、呉橋から表参道の神橋までを月瀬川、表参道神橋から神社の境域付近を浅瀬川といい、場所によって名が変わります。『古事記』、『日本書紀』にある「菟狭の川上」はここのことで、寄藻川沿岸はいろいろな史蹟に富んでいます。
worshipGuide_text_116: 欽明天皇三十二年、八幡大神が御現われになった霊池です。その名の所以は宇佐の三山、菱形にかこまれているため、古くから霊池として有名です。
worshipGuide_text_119: 参集殿・宝物館との間に隣接しているこの池は、奈良の猿沢(さるさわ)の池、京都の広沢(ひろさわ)の池と並ぶ日本三沢の池として古くから有名です。７月～８月にかけて古代蓮が美しく咲きます。
worshipGuide_text_122: 勅使奉幣祭をはじめ大祓式などの祭典の祓の儀を行う所です。この前の広場を古くより御輿掛(おこしかけ)と称し、宮司の輿を倚するところです。
worshipGuide_text_126: 天児屋根命（あめのこやねのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_127: 一之御殿、八幡大神の脇殿です。ご祭神の天児屋根命(あめのこやねのみこと)は春日大明神とも言われ、神功皇后をお助けになった尊神です。
worshipGuide_text_130: 表筒男命（うはづつのおのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_131: 中筒男命（なかつつのおのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_132: 底筒男命（そこつつのおのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_133: 三之御殿、神功皇后の脇殿。神功皇后に数々のご神威を与えられた住吉大神を弘仁１４年(８２３年)よりお祭りしています。
worshipGuide_text_135: じょうぐう ほんでん
worshipGuide_text_137: 宇佐神宮の建築様式は八幡造(はちまんづくり)とよばれています。奥殿を「内院」、前殿を「外院」といいます。内院には御帳台があり、外院には御椅子が置かれ、いずれも御神座となっています。御帳台は神様の夜のご座所であり、椅子は昼のご座所と考えられています。
worshipGuide_text_139: 下宮の八幡大神は、御饌(みけ)を司るとともに、農業や一般産業の発展、充実をお守りになるご神威を発揮されます。古くから日常の祭祀には、とくに国民一般の祈願や報賽(ほうさい)が行われてきました。
worshipGuide_text_142: 応神天皇の若宮であられる大鷦鷯命(仁徳天皇)と皇子をお祀りしています。除災難・厄難の神様として有名です。
worshipGuide_text_145: 鎌倉時代より以前からある西参道の屋根がついた神橋です。昔、呉の国の人が掛けたともいわれ、この名があります。
worshipGuide_text_148: 神宮内郭の南正門。高良大明神、阿蘇大明神の二神を御門の神としてお祀りしています。
worshipGuide_text_151: 比売大神の脇殿といわれ、本宮の地主神と伝えられる造化三神を祀っています。上宮西中門の中に鎮座しています。
worshipGuide_text_154: 文禄のころ（１５９２～）改築されたといわれ、国宝の本殿・勅使門などと共に宇佐神宮の景観を象徴する建物です。
worshipGuide_text_157: 宇佐古来の形式をもつもので、額束(がくづか)はなく、台輪を柱上に置いています。宇佐の鳥居の規格となるものです。
worshipGuide_text_160: 祭器具等を納める高倉の板倉です。前面のみ持送りで縁を設け、擬宝珠(ぎぼし)高欄を備えています。
worshipGuide_text_163: 武内宿禰（たけのうちのすくねのみこと）
worshipGuide_text_164: 武内宿禰は、景行天皇、成務天皇、仲哀天皇、応神天皇、仁徳天皇と、五代の天皇に二百四十余年もの間大臣として仕えたと伝えられます。数多くの功労があり、忠誠を尽くされたことをもってお祀りされています。八幡大神にご奉仕された神であり、古くから大鳥居の外に鎮座になって大神をお護りされています。長寿・忠誠・奉仕などの高いご神徳を授けられます。
worshipGuide_text_165: モノレール のりば
worshipGuide_text_166: 宇佐神宮では、体の不自由な方・高齢の方・ベビーカーのご家族様により良いご参拝が出来ますように無料でモノレールの運行を行っています。ご利用時間：午前8時～午後4時30分。乗車定員：定員6名または定員4名＋車イス1台。※車イス2台の乗車不可。※危険防止のため着席または手すりにおつかまり下さい。

aboutDeities_text_16: 東大寺の大仏建立や道鏡の神託事件の時など、数々のご神威をあらわし皇室を護られたことで朝廷から厚く信仰されてきました。また皇室だけでなく、清和源氏をはじめ全国の武士も武運の神「弓矢八幡」として崇敬を寄せ、一般の人々にも鎮守の神として親しまれてきました。６世紀、菱形池のほとりに初めてご顕現された八幡大神は「誉田天皇広幡八幡麿」また「護国霊験威力神通大自在王菩薩」と名乗られたと伝えられています。仏教の世界でも八幡大菩薩として崇（あが）められ、元寇の時に神風をかせた神は八幡様であるとされています。
aboutDeities_text_17: 「護国霊験威力神通大自在王菩薩」の名の通り自在なる御働きをお顕（あらわ）しになります。また、「八幡神」をお祀りしている八幡神社は全国に存在し、八百万（やおよろず）の広がりを持つ強いご神力でご守護されています。
aboutDeities_text_19: 天照大御神（あまてらすおおみかみ）と素戔嗚尊（すさのおのみこと）の誓約によって誕生したとされる神で、多岐津姫命（たぎつひめのみこと）・市杵嶋姫命（いちきしまひめのみこと）・多紀理姫命（たぎりひめのみこと）の三女神のことを言います。筑紫の宇佐嶋（宇佐の御許山）に天降られたと伝えられており、八幡様のあらわれる以前の古い神様、地主神であるとされています。
aboutDeities_text_20: 海北の道中の主として筑前（福岡）の宗像大社や宮地嶽神社、安芸（広島）の厳島神社などにも祀られており、学問・芸術の上達から財運や交通安全（航海の安全）など幅広くご守護をされています。
aboutDeities_text_22: 神功皇后は、香椎（かしい：福岡県）の斎宮（いわいのみや）で天神地祇（てんじんちぎ）の教えを仰いで熊襲（くまそ）を鎮められた後、皇祖の神助（しんじょ）を得て大陸との交渉を成し遂げられました。『日本書紀』には皇后の敬神（けいしん）の様子や、後に宇美（うみ：福岡県）でご誕生になる応神天皇が胎中天皇であられたこと等が記されています。
aboutDeities_text_23: 神功皇后は母神として神人交歓、安産、教育などの守護をされており、そのご威徳が高くあらわれています。
